#Text=Of all the methods of home food preservation, freezing is one of the simplest and least time-consuming.
#Text=The natural colors, fresh flavors, and nutritive value of most fruits and vegetables are maintained well by freezing.
#Text=However, to freeze foods successfully—that is, to preserve their quality—produce must be carefully selected, prepared and packaged, and properly frozen.
#Text=A steam-pressure canner can be used if it is deep enough.
#Text=For this purpose, set the cover in place without fastening it.
#Text=Be sure the petcock is wide open so that steam escapes and pressure is not built up.
#Text=Standard home canning jars are used for pickles and relishes.
#Text=Do not use jars and lids from commercially canned foods.
#Text=They are designed for use on special packing machines and are not suitable for home canning.
#Text=Select jars free from nicks, chips or any defects.
#Text=As you wash the jars in warm soapy water and rinse them, run your finger around the jar opening to see if there is a defect.
#Text=If there is, the jar will not seal.
#Text=Look at each new metal lid to be sure the sealing compound is even and smooth.
#Text=Check the metal screw band to see that it is not bent or rusty.
#Text=Bands can be used over and over again.
#Text=As for pretreatment of lids and bands, follow the manufacturer’s directions.
#Text=Read these even if you have used that brand before; the directions may have changed.
#Text=Drying time varies according to fruit or vegetable type, size of pieces, and tray load.
#Text=Dehydrator drying generally takes less time than oven drying.
#Text=Sun drying takes considerably more time.
#Text=Before testing foods for desired dryness, remove a handful and cool for a few moments.
#Text=Foods that are warm or hot seem softer, more moist, and more pliable than they will when cooled.
#Text=Foods should be dry enough to prevent microbial growth and subsequent spoilage.
#Text=Dried vegetables should be hard and brittle.
#Text=Dried fruits should be leathery and pliable.
#Text=For long term storage, home dried fruits will need to be drier than commercially dried fruits sold in grocery stores.
#Text=Sugar, salt and vinegar play important roles in quality canned foods.
#Text=While sugar is not needed to safely can fruits, it contributes to better color, flavor and texture.
#Text=Likewise, vegetables may be safely canned without salt, but when salt is used, better flavor results.
#Text=Salt is essential for slow process pickles; vinegar is needed for quick process pickles.
#Text=Canning or pickling salt should always be used.
#Text=Iodized salt will inhibit proper fermentation and react with the starch in some foods, causing them to turn pink or blue.
#Text=Pickles or relishes can add zip and zest to your meals, snacks and party refreshments.
#Text=They contain small amounts of nutrients, depending on ingredients used in making them.
#Text=But they have little or no fat and are low in calories, except for the sweet varieties.
#Text=Sun-drying, salting, smoking and pickling were methods used in ancient times for preserving food.
#Text=Pickling is still popular today.
#Text=Pickling is preserving foods in vinegar or brine or a combination of the two.
#Text=Other ingredients are sometimes added to make pickles crisp and spicy.
#Text=Relishes and some pickles can be made in a few hours.
#Text=Other pickles may take three to six weeks.
#Text=Canning in the home is increasing in popularity as a method of food preservation.
#Text=Economic considerations are causing consumers to look for ways to stretch their food dollars.
#Text=By having their own gardens and canning the harvested produce, they often can save money.
#Text=Eating quality of home-canned products encourages some individuals to can.
#Text=The activity of growing or obtaining produce and preserving it in the home sometimes gives people a sense of personal achievement and satisfaction.

#Text=In canning, food is preserved by applying heat to prepared food in containers so that micro-organisms that cause spoilage or food poisoning are destroyed and enzymes that cause undesirable quality changes in the flavor, color, and texture of food are inactivated.
#Text=Preservation of food by canning also depends on sealing the food in sterile, airtight containers to prevent it from coming in contact with micro-organisms in the environment.
#Text=Canning is not a difficult technique, but it must be done properly to avoid spoilage and food poisoning, such as the often fatal botulism.
#Text=It is extremely important that only tested reliable instructions are used, such as those found in U.S.
#Text=Department of Agriculture publications.
#Text=Based on scientific research, specific instructions have been developed for preparing, packing, and processing each food.
#Text=Instructions should be followed exactly from beginning to end—without taking any shortcuts or altering any recipes.
#Text=Acid content varies among fruits and is higher in underripe fruits.
#Text=Acid is needed both for gel formation and for flavor.
#Text=When fruits are low in acid, lemon juice or citric acid may be used.
#Text=Commercial pectins also have added acid.
#Text=Either beet or cane sugar in fruit products acts as a preserving agent, helps in forming the gel, and enhances the finished product’s flavor.
#Text=In preserves, sugar aids in firming the fruit or fruit pieces.
#Text=Sweeteners such as brown sugar, sorghum and molasses are not recommended since their flavor overpowers the fruit flavor and their sweetness varies.
